speaker: interviewer
text: Emil, number four.

speaker: interviewer
text: You were telling us about the--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, and we were over there in [GERMAN] in Berlin. The--

speaker: interviewer
text: Which year was that?

speaker: interviewee
text: This was in-- in '47. In '47. So they decided all-- all the DP people with small children, they will take him to the DP camps in Germany on the American zone. My-- so I had my daughter, my older daughter. She was in-- she was, in that time, she was seven or eight years old. So-- so they-- so they took us and they brought us to Germany to a place they called this Eschwege. Eschwege at the Flugplatz means over there was-- they had over there planes during the war over there. They were over there Flugplatz.

speaker: interviewer
text: Airport?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Not a airport. The army was over there. The-- the air-- what do you call this? The--

speaker: interviewer
text: A base?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, a base. They had over there the base, and they had over there barracks all over. And those barracks, we were about 3,000 [INAUDIBLE] DP camps, DP people over there in-- in Eschwege.

speaker: interviewer
text: Eschwege was where?

speaker: interviewee
text: It's by Castle. By Castle. I was-- like I was in my youth, I was very active. I was [NON-ENGLISH]. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] I saw what's going on in the world. I decided something has to be done to make the Jewish people free and let them to get their own [INAUDIBLE]. I was-- I was a revisionist, I was. And I decided-- and I decided you can say I'm a chauvinist. I have to say the Jewish people are capable people. The Jewish people are good people, not because I'm saying this. I would-- there is-- there is bad people between us too. I wouldn't-- I wouldn't deny that. Just the majority people we brought up to respect everybody, to help, not just your own brothers and sisters, even your neighbors, not Jews. And if you have-- and if you are capable, we are maybe one thousandth of the world's population. What we did contribute to the welfare of the-- of the human beings, what we will contribute, we have over 10 and a half percent of the all Nobel Prize winners are-- are Jews. Are Jews. We did it because-- because we were-- we were brought up this way.

speaker: interviewer
text: So with that in mind, when you were in Eschwege, you became very active.

speaker: interviewee
text: I became very active. And I worked and I tried to make their life as much easier as I could. We were taken care by the American Army.

speaker: interviewer
text: That's your wife?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no, no, no, no. The whole camp, all the Jews, all the DP camps, they were fed by the Jewish-- by the American Army, occupation army. And the Joint was also over there. The Joint gave clothing and everything. The Joint this, this part. I was active over there. I try to-- I tried to-- I want to give you just an example. In the beginning, we had kitchens. And we had dining rooms. It's 3,000 people. And they were separated for children dining rooms and for this.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I decided that when I went to the American-- yeah, they were a major who took care of-- of our camp. So I went to-- they brought us lard in barrels. So [LAUGHS] we couldn't eat it. So I went to the-- Why couldn't you?

speaker: interviewee
text: In that time, we ate-- you see, when I was in hiding, I ate everything. I ate. I'm not religious. I ate everything. Just fresh butter is better than-- than lard. So I went to the-- to the mayor in the town, and I made with him a deal. I switched. I gave him the lard, and he gave us-- they had over there a dairy factory, and they gave us fresh-- fresh butter. I also did it-- I made out with him [INAUDIBLE] the kitchens. I gave each family their food. We were-- each family had only one room. And sometimes they were four, six people to one room. I still-- I tried to give them-- they should be by themselves and not their own three times a week over there [INAUDIBLE]. I was very, very active, as I was at home, before the war. I was active. Jabotinsky came to-- to our big town, to Rovno, not far from us. It's about 30 some kilometers. I went over there to his speech. I want to mention one thing. I didn't know exactly in the world they are the three best speaker. It was Trotsky, Jabotinsky-- if I don't make a mistake-- Mussolini. Well, I don't know. Those, they are the best. If you could hear Jabotinsky talking, how do you-- how you didn't know nothing, you were ready to follow him. And he said he knew it was through. And he told them, leave the-- leave the Galut. If not, the Galut will-- will finish you. And I wish we would-- we would listen to him. Maybe there would have been more survivors today.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember what year did you go to hear Jabotinsky?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't remember. I don't remember.

speaker: interviewer
text: But that was when you were a student in high school?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no, no. This was already when I was already self-employed already. This was already when I was already in the twenties, I was already.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, I was very, very active.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, so you are in-- in Eschwege, in Castle, and that's 1947?

speaker: interviewee
text: In 1947 and 1948 we were over there. And later on-- and later on they decided to liquidate there.

speaker: interviewer
text: That camp?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: DP?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Because-- because they gave-- people starts leaving. Some went to Israel. Do you know on the-- not the-- because in that time, there was the British mandate, and they didn't let us. And they were-- and they were going anyway, without asking them. So many from-- many left for Israel. My wife did it. And I was ready to go to Israel, and I wanted to go. So my wife had. My younger daughter was born in Eschwege in 1947. She was born in Eschwege. So my wife--

speaker: interviewer
text: What's her name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Her name is Bina. So my wife-- I didn't know I had cousins got in touch from the United States, got in touch with us. And they send us up a package, and they send us papers. We should come to the United States. And my wife insists she wants to go to the-- to the United States. Because her first cousin, what we took her apartment, it so happen my wife was born after 23 years. And the cousin whose apartment we got, her mother died. And my wife's mother, she didn't have children. She took her in and she brought her up.

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh, that cousin?

speaker: interviewee
text: That cousin.

speaker: interviewer
text: You remember her name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Her name is-- her name was-- well what is it? Let me--

speaker: interviewer
text: It will come later.

speaker: interviewee
text: So she did insist. So we came here to the-- to the states. And I wanted to show you what kind of a person I am. All the DPs who came here that [INAUDIBLE] took care of them, put them in in hotels, gave them later apartments, gave them money. When I came here, the cousins-- I had cousins, my wife cousins. They-- they helped us, and I told myself I will not take up any [INAUDIBLE] from the-- from the Joint. So I help my wife's first cousin at-- at the shop. He made dresses, a shop. He was a-- a contractor. And I decided I want to build my own [INAUDIBLE], so as to support my family. They took me in. I never worked physically all my life, and he made me a presser. And this was a very, very hard job. In the shop, a presser, we had steam irons. The temperature could reach 120 degrees.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I worked for him. And then I decided regardless, he was my cousin. OK, I couldn't compete with him. We used to work piecework. I couldn't compete with the Italian fellow presser. I made once. I made not bad, not bad in the beginning. I made $50 or $40. I don't-- I don't remember. One time, I remember, there was a [INAUDIBLE], it was such easy. I made $100, $150. I make, at that time, a millionaire, I thought. So I decided later on, it's not for me. I have too much energy to stay and be-- and be a presser. So I went and I bought-- yeah, I had a friend [INAUDIBLE], whom-- whom I became friendly. He went-- yeah, we came to the states in 1949 in September or October. Where did you leave from?

speaker: interviewee
text: We left from Bremerhaven. Yeah, we-- yeah, I'm going to-- when they liquidated Eschwege, they send us to a DP camp where they call this Wildflecken, a wild place, Wildflecken. Over there, we were in their DP camp.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where was that?

speaker: interviewee
text: This was in Germany. I don't remember exactly where. [NON-ENGLISH- or where, I don't know. I don't remember. And from over there later, I was waiting till the papers came in. And they came-- and it came by-- and they-- and they told me I can go to the United States. So from over there, I went to Bremerhaven. And from Bremerhaven, a military ship took us three to the United States. And we came to New Orleans with the boat. And over there in New Orleans, the Jewish-- Jewish center over there, they came, they greet us, it was. And they took us down. And-- and we stayed over there. And over there, there were already tickets from my cousin to come to New York. So from over there, we came to New York.

speaker: interviewer
text: What kind of tickets?

speaker: interviewee
text: They were railroad tickets.

speaker: interviewer
text: Railroad tickets?

speaker: interviewee
text: And we came by train from New Orleans. We came by train to New York. We were over there in New Orleans a day or two. They took care of us, and they were very nice. They showed us the town, and they fed us. And I came-- we came to New York. And I decided I cannot be a-- a presser, regardless. Regardless that cousin was very nice to us, really. So-- so I decided I'll-- I'll become my own contractor. And with this-- with this friend whom I became friend, [INAUDIBLE], he was already-- he came two years before me. I thought he-- he spoke English. I thought he's a Yankee already. I didn't know what.

speaker: interviewee
text: So we decided, yeah. In the [NON-ENGLISH], in the camp, there was a guy who was from home. He was a tailor. He had golden hands. He could be-- he could be a designer. So I took him, and we bought a shop in Brooklyn. In Brooklyn, we bought a shop, and we worked like horses. What was the name of the shop?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, a contract. We had only 12 operators, that's all. 12 operators, and we worked the presses. And we went-- and me contractor. The job I gives you already, [INAUDIBLE], we have dress to sew together and press it and everything. The older guy was from home like-- like we was. He was a [NON-ENGLISH]. Do you know, with [? water, ?] so-- a tailor--

speaker: interviewer
text: That's a merchant with wood?

speaker: interviewee
text: A schneider at home was considered [? luck ?] like us. So he starts fighting with him. And the schneider was right. So I kept his. So we decided we're going to split. So either we'll give him-- either he'll give each a $1,000, and he'll keep the shop, and we will-- or we will give him.

speaker: interviewee
text: So we decided we'll take the 1,000. Now, I went with the schneider, it's not-- I went with the schneider to look for a shop. The schneider didn't have money. I didn't have. Just I have cousins who would go to the bank and sign for us and get the money. We went and we saw a shop in Brooklyn with 35 operators.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I went in for [INAUDIBLE]. To become contractors, I had a different mind taking [INAUDIBLE] schneiders and make to order for suits and coats, because he was a designer. He could-- everything for a lousy $500. I didn't buy the shop. This was my biggest mistake. But I didn't know it would be. Just-- just I don't regret I have beautiful children. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And as I was not nice to my father, so-- so are they fine. I don't need it. I don't take [INAUDIBLE] [CRYING]. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Jews, there is no doubt-- [PAUSES FOR 9 SECONDS] And by and by, they will never let us in the [INAUDIBLE]. If I needed, there is-- there is no doubt. And so are my two son I lost. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] So are my two son I lost.

speaker: interviewer
text: There is anything you would like to tell your-- your children and grandchildren and future generations? Any advice that--

speaker: interviewee
text: I want to tell you only one thing. I want to tell the-- not just the Jewish, to all, to all the young-- the young people. They have-- God gave them brains. God gave them talent. And they have to know one thing, they came into this world for a purpose. For a-- for a purpose. They should use their talent. They should use their brains to make a better world for the next generation, as the previous generation did for me and for them. And I want to also-- also tell them, God created all the people in the same way. Everybody's equal. Everybody's equal, regardless of race, regardless of color of the skin, of religion-- of religion. And I want to warn them, don't let nobody poison your mind. Don't hate people. Hatred is poison. Poison. They are-- they are bad people. They still are follow it. So you-- you [NON-ENGLISH]. You still of-- of Hitler. There are still racists. And I want to-- I want to just beg them, use your talents. Use your brains for make a better world for the whole humanity, for-- for everybody. Don't-- don't let nobody poison your-- your mind. And I'm positively the Jewish people, regardless what did happen, regardless of the persecution during-- during thousands of years, they will still use their talents and their brain for the welfare of the whole world, regardless. Regardless what they did to us, we will still do it, because it's in, now, our blood. It's in our-- it's in our bringing up. We-- we were brought up to help your neighbor, be nice to your-- to your neighbor. I'm want to just-- I didn't mention after-- after I didn't buy the shop. So I went, I bought a coin laundromat. It was a service one. Me, my wife, we worked 18 hours a day Monday, Saturday. Those was the two busiest days. My older daughter came and help us. I took the dirty-- the dirty laundry sometimes for everybody, and I washed this and fold it. I washed this and fold it. And we worked hard. And if I-- and if I-- and I had to not be-- and I was forced to learn how to fix the machine. Otherwise, I couldn't exist. And if I did accomplish a fix, the pleasure what I had. The same thing is with you. If you will-- if you will do your share, make a better world, you have no idea the pleasure what you will enjoy knowing you did something for-- for your children, for the future generation, for the humanity, what you-- what you did. And stay away from-- [LAUGHS] from drugs. And-- and don't try to have fun all your life. Fun is good, just not-- the whole life cannot be fun. You have to-- you have to do your share. You have a-- a responsibility. Contribute your share for a better world, for you, for your children, for everybody.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you. You told me before that you write poetry.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes. Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Would you like to share?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. I want to-- I want to read you-- I brought many [INAUDIBLE]. It's impossible to read them all. I want to read you one, [YIDDISH]. This means Friday Night when Shabbos Come.

speaker: interviewer
text: When was it written? Do you remember?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was-- it was written maybe eight or 10-- or 10 years ago.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, and this is in Yiddish?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yiddish. I write only Jewish poetry.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. [SPEAKING YIDDISH] Thank you very much. That's very beautiful. Would you like to show us some pictures of your family?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Yeah, you want to-- you want to take them?

speaker: interviewer
text: Sure, we'll bring them here.

speaker: interviewee
text: Tell him to bring everything here.

speaker: interviewer
text: Steve?
